item.title: Top cops visit schools, deliver sporting equipment
item.description: BEING a police officer can leave lasting impressions on families and communities, says Western Highlands police commander, Chief Supt John Sagom. Sagom visited schools in Mt Hagen with Australian Federal Police (AFP) team leader for the Highlands, Insp James Armstrong last week.
item.content:encoded:
BEING a police officer can leave lasting impressions on families and communities, says Western Highlands police commander, Chief Supt John Sagom.

Sagom visited schools in Mt Hagen with Australian Federal Police (AFP) team leader for the Highlands, Insp James Armstrong last week.

Sagom said policemen and women who practised good ethics, high moral values, discipline and code of conduct would leave behind lasting imprints.

He said policing was a career path which students could choose, and interest must start at the early stages of life.

Sagom and Armstrong visited Mun Primary School, Pit Primary School, and Manimp Junior High.

Armstrong, through the PNG-Australia Policing Partnership (PNG-APP), distributed sporting equipment to the three schools in the rural areas of Mt Hagen District.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: UK seeks to operationalise Shiprider agreement
item.description: THE United Kingdom (UK) is looking for opportunities to operationalise the UK-Papua New Guinea Shiprider Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) following its signing in September last year. Deputy defence adviser Lt-Col Matthew Williams said they also wanted to develop their relationship with the PNG Defence Force in strengthening marine capability.
item.content:encoded:
THE United Kingdom (UK) is looking for opportunities to operationalise the UK-Papua New Guinea Shiprider Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) following its signing in September last year.

Deputy defence adviser Lt-Col Matthew Williams said they also wanted to develop their relationship with the PNG Defence Force in strengthening marine capability.

“The Shiprider MoU was signed last year and we’re looking for opportunities to operationalise that MoU and develop our relationship with the PNGDF,” he said.

The agreement sets out how the PNG and UK military personnel could conduct joint operations to enforce maritime laws in the Pacific.

Williams visited the HMS Tamar yesterday with British High Commissioner to PNG Paul Eastwood and ship captain Commander Tom Lindsey. The ship is used for maritime exclusive economic zone enforcement operations.

Lindsey explained that the vessel could provide a boarding and interdiction capability against illegal fishing activity and countering transnational crime and smuggling activity.

He said the HMS Tamar and HMS Spey had been on a mission in the Indo-Pacific region demonstrating a continuous presence of not just the UK defence force but also the UK Government.

“This is my first time here – but not the ship’s. It was here three years ago. Since then, we have covered the entire operating area of Japan all the way down to New Zealand in the south as far west as Malaysia and as far east as Pitcairn Island in the Central Pacific,” he said.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Tightrope walker Jona wows crowd in Kundiawa
item.description: A TIGHTROPE walker in Kundiawa, Chimbu, wowed locals last Friday crossing a traditional cane rope several times at the launch of the province’s Nature, Conservation and Arts Centre. Pantom Jona, 34, from Karamui, did his stunt on the rope, a traditional cane rope known as ‘kanda’ hoisted several metres above the ground.
item.content:encoded:
A TIGHTROPE walker in Kundiawa, Chimbu, wowed locals last Friday crossing a traditional cane rope several times at the launch of the province’s Nature, Conservation and Arts Centre.

Pantom Jona, 34, from Karamui, did his stunt on the rope, a traditional cane rope known as ‘kanda’ hoisted several metres above the ground.

Jona said he had practiced tightrope walking as a boy and spent years master the art. He added that there were five other men on his team who could walk the tightrope.

“It is not magic. Practice makes perfect,” he said while referring to the rope walking art.

He said the skill was a way of life for the people of Karamui when crossing rivers.

He said walking on a single rope to cross rivers and other similar gaps in terrain was actually a skill that many people in the mountainous areas had.

He added that traditional tightrope walking could be developed into a tourist attraction as part of shows and traditional performances.

Jona said he had performed his tightrope walking act three time at Kundiawa Dickson’s Oval and his most recent performance at the Kundiawa Lutheran Day Secondary School.

He said he brought his troupe to the Morobe Show and visited other parts of the Highlands such as Erave in Southern Highlands and Hides and Tari in Hela to perform the act.

Jona said on this occasion he was unable to bring his team because he was unable to cover the cost of air fares from Karamui to Kundiawa.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Marape clarifies Joseph’s ministerial status
item.description: PRIME Minister James Marape told Parliament that only the Defence portfolio was removed from Nipa Kutubu MP Dr Billy Joseph while he remains minister over the other portfolios. Marape said Joseph was defence minister as well as for disaster and emergency services, national volunteer service and Fire Services.
item.content:encoded:
PRIME Minister James Marape told Parliament that only the Defence portfolio was removed from Nipa Kutubu MP Dr Billy Joseph while he remains minister over the other portfolios.

Marape said Joseph was defence minister as well as for disaster and emergency services, national volunteer service and Fire Services.

“Those acts of parliament that administer those functions are under him (Joseph) still. We didn’t not create a new ministry,” Marape said.

Marape was responding to questions by the Opposition Leader James Nomane on the status of Joseph, the Papua New Guinea Defence Force (PNGDF) recruitment saga, and if Marape acting as the minister raised allegations of conflict of interest and lack of governance.

He said 39 cabinet minister hold many portfolios that relate to the jobs and as far as Defence is concerned, Billy is innocent until proper investigations are complete.

“If he’s found to have breached the duties then he will be decommissioned from Defence and other portfolios will take place,” Marape said.

“Three key offices that are under scrutiny are the Office of Defence Secretary, Chief of PNGDF Rear Admiral Philip Polewara and the minister.

“He (Joseph) is minister over portfolios parked under one minister.”

Marape said a prime minister gives ministerial powers to cabinet ministers and at any time the prime minister can assume the role by determination.

“I have assumed the role as defence minister in the meantime when the investigations are running. Thde investigations are almost completed,” he said.

“The Defence Council is constituted by these three offices, but the operation is headed by the commander, that’s where the recruitment comes under.”

Marape added that when the investigation report is complete, those responsible will be dealt with accordingly.

“The report will give us a full detail and composition of recruits that are expected to be completed early next month, that will be made public to bring credibility back to the PNGDF,” Marape said.

He denied having no blood relation to Defence Secretary Hari Akipe and said he (Akipe) was from Chimbu and Hela and served as managing director for Livestock Development Corporation for more than 10 years, giving him that qualification as secretary in his second term.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: NSW stun QLD. Daley’s men pull off great escape in Origin 1
item.description: SYDNEY: The Blues were dead and buried. People wanted Laurie Daley sacked. Fans were questioning whether Nathan Cleary was an Origin player and why James Tedesco was picked over Dylan Edwards. And then they won, doing to Queensland what the Maroons have done to them since the inception of State of Origin as Tedesco soared high to reel in a Cleary bomb to steal the unlikeliest of victories with a m
item.content:encoded:
SYDNEY: The Blues were dead and buried. People wanted Laurie Daley sacked. Fans were questioning whether Nathan Cleary was an Origin player and why James Tedesco was picked over Dylan Edwards.

And then they won, doing to Queensland what the Maroons have done to them since the inception of State of Origin as Tedesco soared high to reel in a Cleary bomb to steal the unlikeliest of victories with a minute to go.

And then they won, doing to Queensland what the Maroons have done to them since the inception of State of Origin as Tedesco soared high to reel in a Cleary bomb to steal the unlikeliest of victories with a minute to go.

It was the “greatest escape” as Mat Thompson put it in commentary, with the 22-20 result in Sydney sending Accor Stadium into delirium as the rain tumbled down.

Cleary, who was named man of the match, produced a second-half comeback mightily similar to the one he conjured in the 2023 Grand Final to lead Penrith to an epic win.

Down 20-0 after 20 minutes, it looked like the Blues were headed for another humbling loss in front of their home fans.

But Cleary and halves partner Ethan Strange flipped the script after a dramatic moment with 23 minutes to go that saw Maroons fullback Kalyn Ponga sent off for a shoulder charge on Blues rookie Tolu Koula.

The halves took control after that with Strange and Cleary both scoring, while the halfback also drilled a 40/20 as he did in the 2023 decider.

It looked like they’d butchered it several times including a shocking pass from Tedesco that was put down by Haumole Olakau’atu when they had a three-man overlap.

But after coming back into the team for the first time in two years, the former NSW skipper justified the selection when he leapt highest to claim a Cleary kick which he juggled but held onto to steal it with one of the most incredible Origin plays.

“The greatest escape,” Mat Thompson declares in commentary.

The final season of Stranger Things was a flop, but Ethan Strange’s Origin debut was a masterpiece for the Blues as he stole the show just a day after he was told that he’d be starting.

It was a chaotic few days for NSW who lost Mitch Moses to a hamstring injury which saw Strange promoted from the bench to play five-eighth.

The Raiders star whacked Cameron Munster to force Queensland’s first error of the night and then had a try taken off him for obstruction, with teammate Cam Murray also denied a four-pointer.

Strange wouldn’t be denied a second time when he backed up a Stephen Crichton break to get his side back into it, and it was his break that should have seen the Blues eat into the deficit when they butchered a certain try.

It was an incredible turnaround by the Blues after an awful 20 minutes, with Mitch Barnett setting the tone from their opening set with a cold drop, while Crichton and Brian To’o were uncharacteristically sloppy with three errors apiece. The Ponga send off completely turned the game on its head, with the Maroons looking set to record another thumping win south of the border when they raced out to a 20-0 lead after 20 minutes.

Sam Walker, already with three man of the match awards on Anzac Day, had a perfect first half as the rookie dominated in the biggest game of his young career.

The Roosters halfback had big boots to fill with reigning Wally Lewis Medal winner Tom Dearden out injured, but he was the best player on the field as Queensland heaped more misery on the men from south of the border.

It was Walker’s grubber that set up the first try of the night for Robert Toia, with the halfback throwing the final pass for their second try after some Harry Grant magic out of dummy-half to set up Tom Flegler for an emotional try in his return to the Origin arena.

Halves partner Cameron Munster grubbered for Hamiso-Tabuai-Fidow to blow the game wide open, but they barely touched it after Ponga’s send off.

They must now win in Melbourne as the Blues did two years ago to keep the series alive to force a decider at Suncorp Stadium on July 8. – Fox League

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Youth academy secures donor support from Aussies
item.description: THE S.O League Agencies and Academy has been named among 23 successful recipients of the Australian government’s inaugural Pilai Long Stap Wantaim (play for unity) Fund. Founder Stanley Olo expressed gratitude for the initiative, telling The National : “Huge credit to the Australian High Commission in PNG for introducing this programme. It’s a big boost for us.
item.content:encoded:
THE S.O League Agencies and Academy has been named among 23 successful recipients of the Australian government’s inaugural Pilai Long Stap Wantaim (play for unity) Fund.

Founder Stanley Olo expressed gratitude for the initiative, telling The National : “Huge credit to the Australian High Commission in PNG for introducing this programme. It’s a big boost for us.

“We recently had Prima partner with us and now, with support from the Australian government, it will help improve our venues and facilities with equipment like balls, cones and other small things we need for our programmes.”

The small grants programme is designed to strengthen community development and youth empowerment in Papua New Guinea through sports.

Olo said the support would enable the academy to continue its work with young players across Port Moresby.

Established in 2024 by the former PNG Hunters, the academy is now in its third year of operations and has quickly gained attention for its weekend rugby league competitions around the nation’s capital.

The academy was recently recognised by the Papua New Guinea Rugby Football League for its contributions to junior rugby league development. Unlike many other programmes, it places emphasis on helping underprivileged children, including those from difficult family backgrounds who often miss out on school sports opportunities.

“Our target is the underprivileged kids,” Olo explained. “Kids from homeless backgrounds, children without proper parenting or from single-parent homes — those are the kids we want to help.”

He added that the programme gives young people hope and a pathway to a better future through rugby league.

“Even if a child does not go to school, rugby league is our national sport and our passion.

“Schoolboy programmes have limited spaces, and that is why we are here for the kids who miss out. Opportunity is here with us at SO Agency.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Former NRL star visits Gulf to promote rugby league
item.description: FORMER National Rugby League player Andrew McCullough visited Gulf to promote the sport in the province. Brought in by the Gulf Province Sports Trust (GPST) through the vision of Gulf Governor Sir Christopher Haiveta, the former Brisbane Broncos star and 300-game veteran, engaged directly with young people and communities.
item.content:encoded:
FORMER National Rugby League player Andrew McCullough visited Gulf to promote the sport in the province.

Brought in by the Gulf Province Sports Trust (GPST) through the vision of Gulf Governor Sir Christopher Haiveta, the former Brisbane Broncos star and 300-game veteran, engaged directly with young people and communities.

This special youth development initiative was supported by Mineral Resources Development Company (MRDC) through Petroleum Resources Kutubu (PRK), Air Niugini, and Boroko Motors.

McCullough arrived in Port Moresby last Thursday from Brisbane ready to share his wealth of knowledge on being an elite sportsman, leadership, and life lessons from the professional sporting arena.

But this visit was about much more than football.

It was about human development, mentorship, discipline, and inspiring the next generation.

McCullough met players and officials from the Gulf Komara football club and the Gulf Isou rugby league team and shared valuable insights and advise.

Last Friday, the team travelled into Gulf and to Malalaua Secondary School, where McCullough conducted a rugby clinic and session with students.

For many students, this was their first opportunity to meet an NRL player face-to-face and the excitement was electric.

Students listened closely as McCullough spoke about believing in yourself, working hard, respecting others, and using sport as a pathway to opportunity.

The journey continued to Kerema on Saturday, where the team attended Ilakaraeta school’s major rugby presentation event, with an incredible crowd of over a thousand students in attendance.

GPST chief executive officer Lawrence Lahari said the initiative reinforced their belief that sport was more than competition – it was a powerful tool for youth development, education, leadership, and community transformation.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Heavies set for double-header showdown
item.description: RUGBY league fans in Lae will be treated to a Round 8 Digicel ExxonMobil Cup double-header at the Lae Rugby League Oval on Sunday, with four team’s eager to strengthen their positions on the competition ladder. The opening clash at 1pm features the Lae Snax Tigers against the Moni Plus NCDC Port Moresby Vipers, followed by the feature game at 3pm between the PRK Mendi Muruks and the Wamp Nga Mt Ha
item.content:encoded:
RUGBY league fans in Lae will be treated to a Round 8 Digicel ExxonMobil Cup double-header at the Lae Rugby League Oval on Sunday, with four team’s eager to strengthen their positions on the competition ladder.

The opening clash at 1pm features the Lae Snax Tigers against the Moni Plus NCDC Port Moresby Vipers, followed by the feature game at 3pm between the PRK Mendi Muruks and the Wamp Nga Mt Hagen Eagles.

The Tigers enter the weekend in 10th place with four points after seven rounds, while the Vipers sit comfortably in fourth on 10 points.

The Tigers will be looking to build on last weekend’s narrow two-point victory over the Gas Resources Central Dabaris, which ended a tough run of results.

Coach Noel Zeming praised his side’s improved discipline and execution, expressing confidence that momentum can carry into this Sunday’s contest.

A win over the higher-ranked Vipers would be a major boost to their campaign.

In the second match, the Muruks — currently third on 10 points — will be aiming to bounce back after last weekend’s loss to the Kroton Hela Wigmen.

The Eagles, meanwhile, sit eighth on six points and will be desperate to secure a valuable win to push into the top half of the ladder.

With crucial competition points at stake, both matches promise intensity and entertainment for supporters at the Lae Rugby League Oval.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Enjoy the game, protect your family
item.description: STATE of Origin is exciting, and there’s nothing wrong with enjoying sport. But when that passion leads to violence, excessive drinking, debt, or even loss of life, we need to question whether it is worth the cost.
item.content:encoded:
STATE of Origin is exciting, and there’s nothing wrong with enjoying sport.

But when that passion leads to violence, excessive drinking, debt, or even loss of life, we need to question whether it is worth the cost.

These games are played between Australian state teams, and while many Papua New Guineans follow them passionately, the results do not improve our roads, hospitals, schools, or job opportunities.

Each year, reports emerge of fights, domestic violence, alcohol abuse, and arrests linked to arguments over football.

Families already struggling with school fees, food prices, and transport costs also spend heavily on betting, alcohol, and merchandise, while betting companies and broadcasters profit.

Sport can unite people in positive ways, but it should never come at the expense of family stability, personal safety, or community peace.

Enjoy the game if you want, but remember that the future of your family and your country matters far more than any scoreline.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: No tolerance for child trafficking
item.description: CHILD trafficking is one of the gravest crimes affecting Papua New Guinea because it exploits the most vulnerable members of society for profit. When children are stolen, sold, or abused, the damage extends beyond individual victims and weakens the moral foundation of entire communities.
item.content:encoded:
CHILD trafficking is one of the gravest crimes affecting Papua New Guinea because it exploits the most vulnerable members of society for profit.

When children are stolen, sold, or abused, the damage extends beyond individual victims and weakens the moral foundation of entire communities.

No level of poverty or personal gain can justify such exploitation, and the law must respond firmly.

Those involved in trafficking make a deliberate choice to place greed above human dignity and public safety.

Lenient punishments only encourage these crimes to continue.

Strong penalties, including lengthy prison terms, seizure of criminal assets, and permanent restrictions on working with children, are necessary to deter offenders and dismantle trafficking networks.

Many suffer severe physical and psychological trauma, lose access to education, and remain affected well into adulthood.

Communities also suffer as fear and mistrust grow among families concerned for their children’s safety.

Police, prosecutors, and courts must be properly supported to investigate and prosecute trafficking cases quickly and consistently.

PNG must also maintain a strong international stance against trafficking to prevent criminal networks from exploiting weak enforcement.

A society is judged by how it protects its children.

PNG must send a clear message that child trafficking will never be tolerated and that protecting children remains a national priority above all else.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Prioritise operational staff
item.description: THERE seems to be a growing top-heavy management structure within an airline company, where administrative and executive positions continue to expand while operational and frontline workers face increasing pressure under cost-cutting measures. Frontline employees are the backbone of any operation.
item.content:encoded:
THERE seems to be a growing top-heavy management structure within an airline company, where administrative and executive positions continue to expand while operational and frontline workers face increasing pressure under cost-cutting measures.

Frontline employees are the backbone of any operation.

However, when organisations prioritise management expansion while limiting operational staffing and imposing stricter cost-saving burdens on frontline workers, it creates imbalance within the system.

Such management approaches often lead to reduced staff morale, operational strain, workforce dissatisfaction, and declining service effectiveness.

Cost control should not disproportionately affect the very employees responsible for delivering the airline’s essential services while management layers continue to grow.

Organisational priorities should focus on strengthening operational capacity, supporting frontline personnel, improving efficiency, and ensuring accountability across all levels of management.

A sustainable organisation is built not only from boardrooms and executive offices, but from the commitment and welfare of the workers on the ground who keep operations running every day.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Healthy debate shapes PNG’s future
item.description: PUBLIC discussion about Papua New Guinea’s political leadership reflects a healthy democratic conversation about governance, development, and the nation’s future. Many acknowledge that past governments delivered important investments in infrastructure, health, education, and district development programs that improved services across rural and urban communities.
item.content:encoded:
PUBLIC discussion about Papua New Guinea’s political leadership reflects a healthy democratic conversation about governance, development, and the nation’s future.

Many acknowledge that past governments delivered important investments in infrastructure, health, education, and district development programs that improved services across rural and urban communities.

These contributions deserve fair recognition within PNG’s development history.

At the same time, democracy depends on accountability, leadership renewal, and strong institutions.

A nation is strengthened not by permanent leadership, but by open competition and the emergence of capable new leaders through merit and integrity.

Papua New Guinea’s strength lies in its diverse pool of experienced leaders across all regions and parties.

Figures such as Allan Bird, Gary Juffa, and Kerenga Kua continue contributing to national debate and governance discussions.

Ultimately, PNG’s progress depends on stronger institutions, improved governance, economic confidence, and leadership guided by national interest, competence, and long-term vision rather than personality politics or short-term advantage.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Pacific concerns about militarisation and NZ’s role in it
item.description: NEW Zealand’s government is increasingly eager to promote the buy-in of Pacific nations for closer defence force integration in the region, amid concerns about militarisation of the region. The security environment has been shifting rapidly, and regional defence is becoming more complex, leaving Pacific Islanders wondering if their “Ocean of Peace” is slipping out of their grasp.
item.content:encoded:
NEW Zealand’s government is increasingly eager to promote the buy-in of Pacific nations for closer defence force integration in the region, amid concerns about militarisation of the region.

The security environment has been shifting rapidly, and regional defence is becoming more complex, leaving Pacific Islanders wondering if their “Ocean of Peace” is slipping out of their grasp.

In recent months, the defence and police forces of Australia and New Zealand have been increasing cooperation with counterparts in Pacific countries, including Fiji, Tonga, Samoa and Vanuatu in efforts to combat transnational crime, especially the illicit drug trade. But as a number of Pacific Island countries weigh up signing major bilateral treaties or agreements with the likes of Australia, China and the United States (US), New Zealand has been steadily pushing Pacific regional defence cooperation on a number of fronts.

The communiqué from last October’s South Pacific Defence Ministers Meeting (SPDMM) in Chile is instructive.

The SPDMM – which involves New Zealand, Australia, Chile, Fiji, France, Papua New Guinea and Tonga – notes the leadership role New Zealand has taken on better coordinating regional defence architecture.

The member countries agreed to push for a defence advisor from their collective to be embedded in the secretariat of the Pacific Islands Forum (PIF), whose secretary-general Baron Waqa attended the Chile meeting and appeared to support closer integration.

While the advisor position is yet to be established, the SPDMM is surging ahead with a range of new regional defence initiatives, including developing the Pacific Response Group, under which defence personnel from Australia, Fiji, France, New Zealand and PNG work together to support coordinated humanitarian assistance and disaster relief responses across the Pacific.

A briefing from March’s joint meeting of the defence and foreign ministers of Australia and New Zealand, emphasised how they aim to promote “the sense of integration through Pacific defence forces’ and to ‘enhance the sense of Pacific forces meeting Pacific security needs”.

It also highlighted a keenness to “get more links between SPDMM and PIF so that these voices are heard directly by the region”.

“The briefings reveal New Zealand’s role in integrating and aligning Pacific defence forces alongside a considerable anxiety about regional social licence,” Pacific historian Marco de Jong said.

He said the language being used “speaks to a programme of influence and public relations, calibrated to downplay criticism that New Zealand is contributing to the militarisation of the Pacific”.

A representative with Development Alternatives with Women for a New Era, Maureen Penjueli, who is also a long time advocate for indigenous rights in the region, said there had been a lack of consultation with the wider Pacific Islands region about the new defence tack.

“We’ve seen so much occupation by those in the defence interests area. For example, the Australian National Security College takes a very primary seat at the Pacific Islands Forum on security. We’ve got competing interests, which is the Fusion Centre that’s in Vanuatu,” Penjueli said.

“When you add more and more players to this regional architecture that already has enough players on defence and security, it complicates the governance structure in a way.

“It does not go through the rigour of national consultations, consultations with civil society around some of these bigger significant shifts around defence and security.”

New Zealand is a long-standing contributor to Pacific regional initiatives, and its defence force is well valued in the region, especially in responding to disasters, humanitarian needs, transnational crime and maritime security threats, and also including in training support.

New Zealand’s defence minister Chris Penk, who replaced Judith Collins in the role since March’s 2+2 Ministerial Meeting with Australia, said that New Zealand always sought to adapt its work with Pacific partners to their context, culture and operational needs.

He told RNZ that in order to support closer cooperation between Pacific militaries, members were also looking at a SPDMM Status of Forces Agreement.

“This would provide a common legal framework for personnel to deploy into each other’s countries more easily, strengthening our collective ability to respond to maritime security challenges as well as humanitarian and disaster relief events.”

Asked if New Zealand is contributing to militarisation of the Pacific Islands, Penk said Pacific partners had warmly welcomed the country’s continued presence and partnership in the region

“The New Zealand defence force contributes to regional responses where it is agreed that defence force personnel and assets should be involved, including humanitarian assistance, maritime domain awareness, fisheries patrols, and search and rescue operations.

“As a Pacific military, we are proud to work alongside our Pacific partners to help respond to the challenges facing our region.”

Penjueli warned that militarisation of the region was escalating against the wishes of most Pacific Islands people.

Making things more complicated, she said, was the growing number of security treaties and agreements that Island countries were being drawn to.

She said they were no longer just about defence or security inter-operability, and often included development and economic dimensions, arrangements that “entangled” Pacific countries into wide ranging commitments beyond traditional military and security ties.

Penjueli worried that the interests of the Islands countries themselves were more than ever being buried under broader geopolitical jostling.

“We were told that this is to ready the region in an anticipation, to contain China, and we’re told that this is about the drug trade and the drug war that’s taking place.

“Yet for the Pacific, climate change or the climate crisis, remains our significant issue around security. So, I think the agendas are very different.”

At their last leaders summit, PIF countries signed up to the Blue Pacific Ocean of Peace Declaration, formally committing the region to peace, sovereignty, and climate justice.

However, Penjueli said being a true ocean of peace required demilitarisation and de-escalation – something which she suggested is not the direction that the defence-oriented governments of the region are heading in. – RNZ

item.title: Ex-security guard Zeming graduates with diploma
item.description: THANKS to young sister Penelope who had challenged family members to pursue further studies and graduate from tertiary institutions, Zeming Zeming received not only a Diploma in Business Management but also the top-student award at the International Training Institute graduation on Friday. “In my immediate family, only the two of us have graduated – me and my small sister (Penelope). She had
item.content:encoded:
THANKS to young sister Penelope who had challenged family members to pursue further studies and graduate from tertiary institutions, Zeming Zeming received not only a Diploma in Business Management but also the top-student award at the International Training Institute graduation on Friday.

“In my immediate family, only the two of us have graduated – me and my small sister (Penelope). She had graduated earlier (in 2022) and she used to challenge us to obtain qualifications (from tertiary institutions) as well.”

Zeming, 31, of Zankoa village in Tewae-Siassi district, Morobe used to score good marks in primary school but never became a top student or dux.

“I was never a dux. This is my first time being a dux. I thank God and my family for this.”

Zeming is the third eldest of five siblings – two brothers and three sisters. Their father Mao Zeming is self-employed while Mum Fredah died in 2018.

Zeming did not complete school because of financial issues. So he took on informal jobs to enroll in the diploma course at ITI.

“I didn’t complete my schooling due to school fee issues. I looked for jobs instead. I became a bus driver, security guard and all sorts of informal jobs until this opportunity arose and I grabbed it with both hands.”

His job as a security guard kindled his interest in management.

“I worked as a security and was happy to interact with people. I felt like I could manage their small complaints and issues. It got me interested in the management course.”

He faced challenges during the two years of study.

“The first two years were not easy. I tried my best to go to work and attend classes. And in the end, I achieved this.”

His family motivated him to get his diploma.

“We are a close-knit family. Every time my brother would counsel me. He reminded me of our humble beginnings and not to take education for granted. It was their motivation that kept me going.”

His father’s strict rules on time guided his study routine.

“ I didn’t complete my schooling due to school fee issues. I looked for jobs instead. I became a bus driver, security guard and all sorts of informal jobs until this opportunity arose and I grabbed it with both hands.”

“Focus is one thing that my father talks about every time, reminding us to be early before schedule and all that. He is a very strict man, very strict on time.

“Some things we don’t know about, he encourages us to ask people for help, to be curious. So yes, it was my father’s strictness that had shaped my study routine.”

“I always make sure that I did my homework and my studies and that things are in order.

“The good thing is that, I have a good family. They are very understanding people. Once in a while they would ask me for help. But mostly, they respect me; they played their part and mine as well.”

“My brother is always my role model. He used to remind us that success is there, all you need to do to achieve it is to remain focused.

“He does not have a big job but he ensures that we all are taken care of. He took on the family responsibility and I have respect for him.”

Zeming is happy to have accomplished his goal.

“I am really happy. Seeing (cousin sister) Luddy also graduating today makes me really happy. We helped each other at school. We also help other Morobe students as well and in the end, we made it.”

Zeming dedicated the dux award to his late mother and his diploma to his father.

“I dedicated these achievements to my family, those who had helped me with my school fees and my upbringing. And this dux award is dedicated to my late mum. Dad gets the diploma.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Neidab dedicates degree, award to late father
item.description: WHEN Neidab Ulu went on stage to receive his degree and award, he was oblivious to the cheering, applause and claps as he was missing and thinking of the person he wanted to be there to witness his achievement – his late father. “I lost my father during the first semester last year. He should have been attending and witnessing this ceremony.”
item.content:encoded:
WHEN Neidab Ulu went on stage to receive his degree and award, he was oblivious to the cheering, applause and claps as he was missing and thinking of the person he wanted to be there to witness his achievement – his late father.

“I lost my father during the first semester last year. He should have been attending and witnessing this ceremony.”

Neidab lost his father Ulu Rusini last year during the second semester of his final year – a critical time for any final year university student.

However, he managed to pull through and graduated with a Bachelor in Public Finance and Accountancy degree.

Neidab, 39, from Wanigela Village, Abau District, Central, is the second eldest of six children.

He was among those who graduated from the University of PNG School of Business and Public Policy last Thursday.

To his employer, the Public Service Commission, he was an investment which paid off when he not only graduated but also named the top performing student in the Bachelor of Public Finance and Accountancy class.

He received the Academic Excellence Award after being sponsored by the PSC, where he has worked for 11 years, to pursue the degree while working as the acting Senior Budget Officer and Certifying Officer.

It was an opportunity he took seriously.

“I thank my employer, the Public Service Commission, for the investment and opportunity and trust placed in me to send me for further studies.”

His work involves budget support, expenditure management, and financial advisory duties. He knows that the degree will help him in his work and lift his performance.

“What motivated me to go back for further studies was to enhance my skills in my current duties on the processes in place to manage the public funds effectively and efficiently.”

Neidab attained a Certificate in Business Studies in 2007 and a Diploma in Business Studies in 2008 at the Port Moresby Business College.

In 2023, he graduated with a Diploma in Accounting from the Somare Institute of Leadership and Governance (SILAG).

The loss of his father affected him. On top of that, he faced financial issues and tried his best to juggle his time between work, study, and family.

“I encountered financial challenges attending classes, attending to family matters, as well as work.”

At times when work and study demanded his attention most, he prioritised which one to tackle first.

“I’ve had clashes between my studies and my job. Some time, I had to attend to my work first and then my studies second because work is my bread and butter.”

The experience changed how he managed his time.

“It shaped me as a person to think critically and handle issues in a way that is balanced.”

At home, his wife Wendy Ulu and two sons Neidrolson who is in Grade Six, and Braden in Grade 4, endured nights when he had to burn the midnight oil.

Wife Wendy remembers those nights when they hardly went to sleep as Neidab would spend hours studying and keeping the lights on.

So she was happy and relieved to see him graduate with flying colours – a fitting reward for his hard work and perseverance. For the family, the hours of struggling to sleep are finally over.

Neidab dedicated the award to his employer, his late father, and his family.

“I dedicate the award to my employer and to my father who should have attended and witnessed this ceremony. And not forgetting my family who supported me throughout this journey.”
